metadata.title: Texas P&C Practice Exam: 25'-26' - Essential Timeline Edition
metadata.exam_scope: Texas General Lines Property & Casualty Agent only. Includes only the required Pearson/TDI P&C general knowledge and P&C agent state-specific scope, plus essential timeline/number traps that fit that scope.
metadata.format_note: Each item has four answer choices plus an 'insight' field intended to be shown after answering. Questions avoid 'all of these except' structure.
primary_sources: Pearson VUE Texas Insurance Content Outlines, publication 124401, effective December 1, 2025: Property and Casualty - General Knowledge and Property and Casualty Agent - State Specific only.
Texas Department of Insurance - General lines property and casualty license application and management pages.
Texas Department of Insurance - Continuing education information for agents and adjusters.
Texas Department of Insurance - Auto insurance guide.
Texas Department of Insurance - Home insurance guide.
Texas Department of Insurance - Surplus lines consumer guide, used only for the P&C Agent outline's surplus-lines topic, not for the separate Surplus Lines exam.

P&C Academy cheat-sheet article used only as a non-official checklist for possible timeline/number traps; only essential Texas General Lines P&C scope items were kept and Texas-specific facts were checked against official sources where possible.
metadata.source_caution: This is a study aid, not legal advice. For exact statutory wording, use the Texas Insurance Code, Texas Administrative Code, current TDI pages, and current Pearson candidate materials.
metadata.revision_note: V6 narrows the V5 timeline add-ons to essential exam-relevant items, removes close-call wording from the added timeline section, and keeps the Codex practice exam aligned with the source material.
removed_as_not_required_for_p_and_c_agen: Standalone Surplus Lines exam scope such as Stamping Office, purchasing groups, risk retention groups, reinsurance, surplus-lines application prerequisites, and surplus-lines-only operational details.
Standalone Personal Lines exam sections beyond topics already included in the broader P&C general knowledge outline.
Standalone Risk Manager, Managing General Agent, All Lines Adjuster, Property & Casualty Adjuster, Public Adjuster, Limited Lines, Life, Health, and HMO exam sections.
metadata.scope_warning: Do not train this as a Surplus Lines, Risk Manager, MGA, Adjuster, Personal Lines, Limited Lines, Life, Health, or HMO exam guide. License-role names such as surplus lines agent, managing general agent, adjuster, risk manager, limited license, and emergency license appear only because they are listed inside the Texas P&C Agent State Specific outline.

questions.insight: Formal definition: a contractual risk-transfer mechanism. Elements: premium, covered risk, policy terms, insurer promise, and insured duties. Important because nearly every P&C question assumes this risk-transfer purpose. Close-call: not the same as a warranty or maintenance plan; insurance addresses fortuitous loss, not ordinary wear.
questions.question: Which term best describes the transfer of financial risk from a person or business to an insurer in exchange for premium?

questions.insight: Formal definition: as the number of similar exposure units increases, actual losses tend to approach expected losses. It matters for rating, underwriting, and spread of risk. Close-call: it does not predict exactly who will have a loss; it improves group-level predictability.
questions.question: Which concept explains why insurers can predict losses more accurately when they insure many similar exposure units?

questions.insight: An exposure unit is the measurable subject of insurance, such as a building, car, employee payroll, or liability operation. It is used to rate premiums and classify risk. Close-call: peril is the cause of loss; exposure is what is subject to loss.
questions.question: Which term describes the person, property, vehicle, operation, or liability possibility that creates a chance of loss?

questions.insight: Risk is the uncertainty that a loss may occur. In P&C, insurers mainly handle pure risk, not speculative risk. Apply it when deciding whether something is insurable. Close-call: risk is not the same as peril; a peril is the cause of loss.
questions.question: Which term means uncertainty about financial loss?

questions.insight: Pure risk has no chance of gain, only loss or no loss, such as fire damaging a house. It is generally insurable. Close-call: speculative risk includes a possibility of gain and is generally not insurable.
questions.question: Which term describes a situation with only two outcomes: loss or no loss?

questions.insight: Speculative risk includes the chance of gain, such as investing or gambling. Insurers generally avoid it because it is not a fortuitous loss exposure. Close-call: pure risk is insurable; speculative risk is generally not.
questions.question: Which term describes a situation with the possibility of loss, no loss, or gain?

questions.insight: Insurable interest means the insured would suffer financial loss if the insured property or exposure is damaged. In property insurance it generally must exist at the time of loss. Important because policies are not meant to be wagers. Close-call: ownership is common but not the only source; leasehold or secured interests may also qualify.
questions.question: Which concept requires the insured to have a financial stake in the property or person being insured?

questions.insight: Adverse selection occurs when riskier applicants are more likely to buy or keep coverage. Underwriting, pricing, exclusions, and waiting periods control it. Close-call: moral hazard is behavior after or during insurance; adverse selection is risk imbalance in the pool.
questions.question: Which concept describes a tendency for people with higher-than-average loss exposure to seek insurance more actively than lower-risk people?

questions.insight: Spread of risk distributes potential loss across many insureds or geographic areas. It supports stable pricing and solvency. Close-call: spreading risk is broader than simply charging premium; the insurer needs enough similar independent exposures.
questions.question: Which principle means an insurer reduces uncertainty by insuring many independent exposures rather than concentrating all risk in one place?

questions.insight: A fortuitous loss is unintended and not certain to occur. It is crucial because insurance is not designed to pay for intentional or inevitable damage. Close-call: wear and tear is expected deterioration, not a fortuitous loss.
questions.question: Which concept means a loss should be accidental or unexpected from the insured's standpoint to be insurable?

questions.insight: A hazard is not the cause of loss itself; it makes a loss more likely or worse. Types on the outline include moral, morale, and physical hazards. Close-call: peril is the cause of loss, such as fire; hazard is a contributing condition, such as oily rags near a furnace.
questions.question: Which term means a condition that increases the chance, frequency, or severity of a loss?

questions.insight: Moral hazard is a character/intent problem: fraud, arson, or intentional misrepresentation. It matters because it may void coverage or trigger criminal/regulatory consequences. Close-call: morale hazard is carelessness, not deliberate dishonesty.
questions.question: Which hazard involves dishonesty or intent, such as an insured deliberately causing or exaggerating a loss?

questions.insight: Morale hazard is a careless attitude, such as leaving a car unlocked because it is insured. It increases loss probability but lacks dishonest intent. Close-call: moral hazard involves intentional or dishonest conduct.
questions.question: Which hazard involves carelessness or indifference because insurance exists?

questions.insight: Physical hazard is a physical condition, such as bad wiring, icy steps, or poor maintenance. Underwriters look for it when pricing or declining risks. Close-call: it is not the same as morale hazard, which is an attitude.
questions.question: Which hazard is a tangible condition that increases the likelihood or severity of loss?

questions.insight: A peril is the event that causes damage. Policy forms may cover named perils or open perils subject to exclusions. Close-call: hazard increases the chance of a peril; it is not the peril itself.
questions.question: Which term means the direct cause of loss, such as fire, windstorm, theft, or collision?

questions.insight: Named perils coverage pays only when the loss is caused by a peril named in the form. It is common in basic or broad forms and personal property coverage in many policies. Close-call: open perils covers direct physical loss unless excluded.
questions.question: Which approach covers only causes of loss that are specifically listed in the policy?

questions.insight: Open perils, also called special form coverage, starts broad and then removes coverage through exclusions. It is often used for HO-3 dwelling coverage and DP-3 dwelling coverage. Close-call: it is not 'all risk' in the literal sense because exclusions still apply.
questions.question: Which approach covers direct physical loss unless the cause is excluded or limited?

questions.insight: A loss is damage, destruction, liability, or financial injury. It can be direct or indirect. Close-call: claim is the demand for payment; loss is the event/financial harm giving rise to the claim.
questions.question: Which term means the reduction in value or financial harm resulting from a covered event?

questions.insight: Direct loss is the actual physical damage, such as fire damage to a building. Important because property policies first ask whether direct physical loss occurred. Close-call: indirect loss is a consequential financial result, such as lost income.
questions.question: Which term describes immediate physical damage to covered property caused by a covered peril?

questions.insight: Indirect loss follows from direct damage, such as loss of use, extra expense, or business income loss after a fire. It usually requires specific coverage. Close-call: the broken building is direct loss; revenue lost while closed is indirect loss.
questions.question: Which term describes a consequential financial loss that results from a direct loss?

questions.insight: ACV generally pays the cost to replace property with like kind and quality less depreciation. It is common for auto total losses and some property losses. Close-call: replacement cost does not subtract depreciation once replacement conditions are met.
questions.question: Which valuation method generally equals replacement cost minus depreciation?

questions.insight: Replacement cost is broader than ACV because it does not reduce payment for depreciation when conditions are satisfied. It is critical in homeowners and commercial property. Close-call: replacement cost is not market value; it focuses on rebuilding or replacing, not sale price.
questions.question: Which valuation method pays the cost to repair or replace damaged property with like kind and quality without deducting depreciation, subject to policy terms?

questions.insight: Market value is the sale price between willing buyer and seller. It may include land, location, and market factors. Close-call: replacement cost is construction/replacement cost; ACV is replacement cost minus depreciation.
questions.question: Which valuation method reflects what property would sell for in the open market?

questions.insight: Stated or agreed value fixes the value shown in the policy or schedule, often for unique property. It matters for autos, fine arts, or specialty property. Close-call: stated amount can still be subject to policy wording; do not assume unlimited coverage.
questions.question: Which valuation method uses an amount agreed on in advance for certain property, often to avoid later valuation disputes?

questions.insight: Salvage value is what damaged property is worth after the loss. Insurers may take salvage after paying the claim, reducing overall loss cost. Close-call: salvage is not depreciation; depreciation is value lost from age/wear before the loss.
questions.question: Which term means the remaining value of damaged property after a loss?

questions.insight: Depreciation is subtracted under ACV settlements. It is central to understanding why ACV checks are lower than replacement-cost estimates. Close-call: deductible is the insured's retained part of a claim; depreciation is value reduction.
questions.question: Which term describes loss in property value due to age, wear and tear, or obsolescence?

questions.insight: Coinsurance encourages insurance to value. Formula idea: carried insurance divided by required insurance times the loss, minus deductible. Close-call: coinsurance in property is not the same as health-insurance cost sharing.
questions.question: Which clause requires the insured to carry insurance equal to at least a stated percentage of property value to avoid a partial-loss penalty?

questions.insight: Insurance to value supports adequate limits and avoids coinsurance penalties. It matters for replacement cost and commercial property. Close-call: it is not the same as market value; policies often focus on replacement cost value.
questions.question: Which concept means carrying limits close to the property's full insurable value so losses are not underinsured?

questions.insight: A deductible reduces claim payment and keeps small losses with the insured. Apply it after determining coverage and valuation. Close-call: a deductible is not a policy limit; limits cap insurer payment.
questions.question: Which term means the amount of a covered loss the insured must absorb before the insurer pays?

questions.insight: Policy limits cap insurer liability. Types include per occurrence, per person, aggregate, and sublimits. Close-call: deductible reduces payment; limit caps payment.
questions.question: Which term means the maximum amount the insurer will pay for a coverage, person, occurrence, or policy period?

questions.insight: A sublimit narrows coverage for a particular category, such as jewelry, money, debris removal, or mold. It matters because the declarations limit may not be the amount available for every loss. Close-call: exclusion removes coverage; sublimit limits covered payment.
questions.question: Which term means a lower limit within the policy for a specific property type, peril, or expense?

questions.insight: Proximate cause determines which peril legally caused the loss when multiple events are involved. It matters in coverage disputes. Close-call: immediate cause may not be the legal proximate cause.
questions.question: Which doctrine identifies the dominant cause that sets a chain of events in motion leading to a loss?

questions.insight: Concurrent causation appears when covered and excluded causes combine. Policies may include anti-concurrent causation language to bar coverage when an excluded peril contributes. Close-call: proximate cause looks for the dominant cause; concurrent causation focuses on combined causes.
questions.question: Which concept involves two or more causes contributing to a loss, sometimes with one covered and one excluded?

questions.insight: Indemnity prevents profit from loss. It underlies ACV, replacement-cost conditions, subrogation, and insurable interest. Close-call: valued policies or agreed value may modify exact indemnity mechanics.
questions.question: Which principle says insurance should restore the insured to the approximate financial position occupied before the loss, not create profit?

questions.insight: Liability is legal responsibility arising from negligence, statute, contract, or certain strict/absolute duties. Casualty insurance pays covered sums the insured is legally obligated to pay. Close-call: liability coverage is third-party; property coverage is usually first-party.
questions.question: Which term means legal responsibility for injury or damage to another person or property?

questions.insight: Absolute liability is imposed because the activity is inherently dangerous, regardless of care used. Examples often include blasting or keeping dangerous animals. Close-call: strict liability may also not require negligence, but exam materials may distinguish by context.
questions.question: Which type of liability can apply without proof of negligence for especially hazardous activities?

questions.insight: Strict liability focuses on responsibility without needing to prove breach of ordinary care. It matters in product claims. Close-call: negligence requires duty, breach, causation, and damages.
questions.question: Which type of liability can impose responsibility without proving negligence, often for defective products or certain hazardous activities?

questions.insight: Vicarious liability shifts responsibility because of a legal relationship. It is common in business auto, employers liability, and general liability. Close-call: direct liability is based on the party's own acts.
questions.question: Which type of liability makes one party legally responsible for another party's acts, such as an employer for an employee acting within scope?

questions.insight: Elements usually tested: duty, breach, proximate cause, and damages. It is central to liability claims. Close-call: strict liability does not require proving breach of duty.
questions.question: Which theory of liability involves failure to use reasonable care, causing harm to another?

questions.insight: Duty is the first negligence element. Without a duty, no negligence liability arises. Close-call: breach is the failure to meet the duty; causation links breach to damage.
questions.question: Which negligence element asks whether the defendant owed a legal obligation to act reasonably toward the claimant?

questions.insight: Breach is the unreasonable act or omission. It matters because a duty alone is not enough for negligence. Close-call: damages must still result from the breach.
questions.question: Which negligence element asks whether the defendant failed to meet the required standard of care?

questions.insight: Damages are the financial or legal harm claimed. Types include compensatory and punitive. Close-call: liability can exist only if harm is tied to the negligent conduct.
questions.question: Which negligence element asks whether the claimant suffered legally recognized harm?

questions.insight: Compensatory damages make the claimant whole. Types include special damages and general damages. Close-call: punitive damages punish or deter and are not intended simply to reimburse.
questions.question: Which damages are intended to reimburse an injured party for actual loss?

questions.insight: General damages compensate intangible harms. They are harder to measure than special damages. Close-call: special damages are measurable economic losses such as medical bills or lost wages.
questions.question: Which compensatory damages are non-economic damages such as pain and suffering?

questions.insight: Special damages can be documented with bills, receipts, or wage records. They are a main component of liability claim valuation. Close-call: general damages are intangible, non-economic harms.
questions.question: Which compensatory damages are measurable economic losses, such as medical bills, repair bills, or lost wages?

questions.insight: Punitive damages are not primarily to reimburse the claimant. Coverage may be limited or prohibited by policy wording or law. Close-call: compensatory damages repay actual loss.
questions.question: Which damages are designed to punish especially wrongful conduct and deter similar conduct?

questions.insight: A binder provides temporary coverage subject to its terms and effective dates. It is important because coverage may begin before the policy is delivered. Close-call: a certificate of insurance is evidence of insurance but usually does not itself create or change coverage.
questions.question: Which term means temporary evidence of insurance coverage pending issuance of the actual policy?

questions.insight: An endorsement changes the policy. It can broaden or restrict coverage and often controls over conflicting printed form language. Close-call: declarations summarize coverage; endorsements amend it.
questions.question: Which document modifies a policy by adding, deleting, or changing coverage or conditions?

questions.insight: Blanket coverage uses one limit over multiple items, locations, or property types. It provides flexibility but requires accurate values. Close-call: specific insurance lists separate limits for specific items or locations.
questions.question: Which coverage approach insures multiple classes or locations under one overall limit?

questions.insight: Specific insurance limits coverage item by item or location by location. It is less flexible than blanket coverage but clearer for valuation. Close-call: scheduled property lists specific items; blanket coverage pools limits.
questions.question: Which coverage approach assigns a separate limit to a particular item, building, class, or location?

questions.insight: Declarations are the policy's information page. Use them first to identify who, what, when, and how much. Close-call: the insuring agreement states the insurer's promise; declarations provide policy-specific data.
questions.question: Which policy section identifies named insured, policy period, coverages, limits, deductibles, premium, and covered property or autos?

questions.insight: The insuring agreement grants coverage, subject to conditions and exclusions. It answers the first question: what does the insurer promise? Close-call: exclusions remove coverage; conditions state duties.
questions.question: Which policy section states the insurer's basic promise to pay or defend covered claims?

questions.insight: Conditions include duties after loss, premium, cancellation, concealment, cooperation, and legal action conditions. Failure to satisfy a condition can affect coverage. Close-call: exclusions describe what is not covered.
questions.question: Which policy section states the rules, duties, and procedures the insured and insurer must follow?

questions.insight: Exclusions narrow coverage that might otherwise exist. Always read exclusions after the insuring agreement. Close-call: limitation may reduce amount or scope; exclusion removes coverage for a category.
questions.question: Which policy section removes coverage for certain causes, property, persons, uses, or situations?

questions.insight: Limitations reduce or qualify coverage. Examples include sublimits, time limits, vacancy limits, and territory restrictions. Close-call: exclusion bars coverage; limitation may leave partial coverage.
questions.question: Which policy wording restricts coverage amount, time, location, or circumstances without always excluding the loss entirely?

questions.insight: Definition of insured determines who receives protection, including named insureds, resident relatives, employees, permissive users, or organizations depending on policy type. Close-call: named insured has broader rights than some additional insureds.
questions.question: Which policy section explains who qualifies for coverage under the policy?

questions.insight: The first named insured often receives notices, pays premiums, and controls certain policy changes. This is critical in commercial policies. Close-call: additional insured receives limited coverage but usually not the same control rights.
questions.question: Which party listed first on the declarations usually has special rights and duties, such as cancellation rights and premium responsibility?

questions.insight: An additional insured is not the original policyholder but receives specified protection. Often used in contracts. Close-call: loss payee/mortgagee has property-payment interests, not the same as liability insured status.
questions.question: Which party is added to a policy to receive certain liability coverage, usually by endorsement?

questions.insight: The named insured has the broadest policy rights and duties. In personal lines this may include spouse/resident spouse depending on form. Close-call: an insured may be covered without being the named insured.
questions.question: Which party is specifically listed in the declarations as the policyholder?

questions.insight: Duties after loss are conditions that help the insurer investigate and adjust claims. Failure can prejudice coverage. Close-call: notice of claim is one duty; proof of loss is a more formal sworn statement when required.
questions.question: Which condition requires the insured to notify the insurer, protect property, cooperate, submit proof, and preserve rights after a loss?

questions.insight: Proof of loss gives the insurer detailed claim information. It matters because claim deadlines and payment obligations may depend on requested information. Close-call: notice of claim is the initial report; proof of loss is detailed support.
questions.question: Which document is a formal statement of the amount and circumstances of loss, often signed or sworn when required?

questions.insight: Notice of claim triggers the insurer's investigation and claim deadlines. Policies usually require notice as soon as practical. Close-call: proof of loss is not the same as initial notice.
questions.question: Which term describes promptly telling the insurer that a loss or claim occurred?

questions.insight: Appraisal is for amount-of-loss disputes, not coverage disputes. Each side usually selects an appraiser; an umpire resolves differences. Close-call: arbitration can resolve broader legal disputes depending on agreement; appraisal focuses on valuation.
questions.question: Which process resolves disputes about the amount of a property loss by using appraisers and an umpire?

questions.insight: Other insurance provisions prevent duplicate recovery and coordinate primary, excess, or pro rata payment. Important for overlapping policies. Close-call: subrogation pursues recovery from a responsible third party after payment.
questions.question: Which provision explains how a loss is paid when more than one policy covers the same loss?

questions.insight: Subrogation supports indemnity by preventing double recovery and shifting loss to the responsible party. Insureds must not impair this right. Close-call: salvage involves damaged property; subrogation involves legal recovery rights.
questions.question: Which principle allows an insurer that paid a loss to pursue recovery from the legally responsible third party?

questions.insight: Mortgagee clauses protect the lender named in the policy. The mortgagee may need to comply with duties after notice. Close-call: loss payee is broader and may not have the same independent protection as a standard mortgagee.
questions.question: Which policy provision protects a lender's insurable interest in covered property, sometimes even if the insured's claim is denied?

questions.insight: Supplementary payments support defense and claim handling. They may be outside limits in many forms, but always check wording. Close-call: damages are sums owed to claimant; supplementary payments are extra claim expenses.
questions.question: Which liability policy feature pays certain claim-related expenses in addition to the limit, such as defense costs or bail bonds, depending on wording?

questions.insight: Consent-to-settle provisions affect who controls settlement. They matter when reputation or professional judgment is involved. Close-call: duty to defend concerns providing defense; consent to settle concerns settlement authority.
questions.question: Which provision requires insurer or insured consent before settling certain claims, often seen in professional liability?

questions.insight: Territory states the coverage area, such as the United States, its territories/possessions, Puerto Rico, and Canada in many personal auto forms. Close-call: location schedule identifies covered property; territory defines geographic scope.
questions.question: Which policy provision defines where coverage applies geographically?

questions.insight: Occurrence triggers many liability policies. It is central to CGL and homeowners liability. Close-call: claims-made coverage is triggered by when the claim is made, not necessarily when occurrence happened.
questions.question: Which term generally means an accident, including continuous or repeated exposure to substantially the same harmful conditions?

questions.insight: Policy period controls whether a loss falls within coverage timing. Claims-made and occurrence forms treat timing differently. Close-call: retroactive date may restrict claims-made coverage even if the claim occurs during the policy period.
questions.question: Which term identifies the beginning and ending dates during which coverage applies?

questions.insight: Cancellation terminates coverage midterm by the insurer or insured under policy and legal rules. It often requires notice and may trigger return premium. Close-call: nonrenewal occurs at the end of the policy period.
questions.question: Which term means ending a policy before its expiration date?

questions.insight: Nonrenewal is not midterm termination; it happens at expiration. Texas rules often require notice and permissible reasons depending on coverage. Close-call: cancellation is before expiration.
questions.question: Which term means the insurer declines to continue coverage after the policy expires?

questions.insight: Vacancy can suspend or limit coverage after a specified period because risk of vandalism, water damage, and theft increases. Close-call: unoccupancy means no people are present, but property may remain furnished or ready for use.
questions.question: Which term usually means a building has no occupants and little or no contents, creating increased property risk?

questions.insight: Unoccupancy is less severe than vacancy. Policies may treat each differently. Close-call: vacancy often means both no occupants and no substantial contents/use.
questions.question: Which term generally means premises are not being lived in or used, though contents may remain?

questions.insight: A valid contract generally requires offer/acceptance, consideration, competent parties, and legal purpose. Insurance policies are contracts, so these elements matter. Close-call: unique insurance characteristics include adhesion, aleatory, unilateral, and conditional.
questions.question: Which concept includes offer and acceptance, consideration, competent parties, and legal purpose?

questions.insight: In insurance, the application and premium may be an offer, and issuance/binding may be acceptance depending on facts. Close-call: consideration is the exchange of value, not the agreement itself.
questions.question: Which contract element is shown when one party proposes terms and the other agrees to them?

questions.insight: Consideration supports the contract. The insured provides premium and representations; the insurer provides promises of coverage. Close-call: legal purpose means the contract objective must be lawful.
questions.question: Which contract element is the value exchanged by the parties, such as premium and statements in exchange for insurer promises?

questions.insight: Competent parties means the parties can legally enter the agreement. It matters for enforceability. Close-call: legal purpose is separate; it asks whether the agreement's objective is lawful.
questions.question: Which contract element requires parties to have legal capacity to contract?

questions.insight: Legal purpose prevents enforcement of illegal agreements. Insurance must cover legitimate insurable interests, not wagers or illegal acts. Close-call: insurable interest supports legal purpose but is also its own insurance principle.
questions.question: Which contract element requires the contract to be for a lawful objective?

questions.insight: Insurance policies are contracts of adhesion because the insured generally accepts the insurer's wording as offered. This matters for interpretation. Close-call: unilateral means only the insurer makes an enforceable promise after premium is paid.
questions.question: Which insurance contract characteristic means the insurer drafts the policy and ambiguous wording is usually interpreted against the insurer?

questions.insight: Aleatory means performance depends on chance and exchanged values can be unequal. Important for understanding insurance as risk transfer. Close-call: commutative contracts exchange approximately equal values.
questions.question: Which insurance contract characteristic means the values exchanged may be unequal because a small premium may result in a large claim payment?

questions.insight: Insurance is unilateral because the insured does not promise to have a loss, but the insurer promises to pay covered losses if conditions are met. Close-call: conditional means policy performance depends on meeting conditions.
questions.question: Which insurance contract characteristic means only one party, the insurer, makes an enforceable promise to pay covered claims?

questions.insight: Conditional means duties and conditions must be met, such as notice and proof of loss. Close-call: unilateral is about who makes an enforceable promise; conditional is about prerequisites to performance.
questions.question: Which insurance contract characteristic means coverage depends on the insured satisfying policy conditions?

questions.insight: A warranty is stronger than a representation and may void coverage if breached, depending on law and wording. Close-call: representation is a statement believed true to the best of the applicant's knowledge.
questions.question: Which statement or promise in insurance must be strictly true or complied with if made part of the contract?

questions.insight: A representation should be truthful and material. False material representations can affect coverage or licensing. Close-call: warranty is generally stricter than representation.
questions.question: Which application statement is made to the best of the applicant's knowledge and used by the insurer in underwriting?

questions.insight: Misrepresentation can void coverage, trigger claim denial, or constitute an unfair trade practice depending on context. Important for Texas marketing rules. Close-call: concealment is hiding material information rather than affirmatively stating it falsely.
questions.question: Which term describes a false statement of material fact that can affect underwriting, claims, or marketing?

questions.insight: Concealment hides material information and can affect policy validity or claim payment. It matters in applications and claims. Close-call: misrepresentation is an inaccurate statement; concealment is omission.
questions.question: Which term means failing to disclose a material fact that should be revealed?

questions.insight: Insurance fraud can be civil, criminal, and regulatory. It includes staged losses, false claims, and fake information. Close-call: error may be accidental; fraud requires intent.
questions.question: Which term involves intentional deception for unlawful gain in insurance applications, claims, or transactions?

questions.insight: Underwriting classifies risk, identifies hazards, and sets eligibility, terms, and premium. Sources may include applications, inspections, loss history, MVR, credit-based insurance score, or CLUE. Close-call: rating calculates price; underwriting decides acceptability and terms.
questions.question: Which process evaluates applicants and exposures to decide whether to insure them and at what rate?

questions.insight: FCRA applies to consumer reports such as credit, MVR, and investigative reports. It requires disclosures/adverse-action procedures. Close-call: GLBA focuses on privacy of nonpublic personal financial information.
questions.question: Which federal law regulates consumer reports and gives consumers rights when reports are used in underwriting?

questions.insight: GLBA privacy rules govern how financial institutions, including insurers, handle nonpublic personal information. Close-call: FCRA governs consumer reports used in eligibility or rating decisions.
questions.question: Which federal law requires privacy protections and notices for nonpublic personal financial information?

questions.insight: The application is a key underwriting source and may become part of the policy. Accuracy matters because statements can be representations. Close-call: declarations are issued with the policy; application precedes or supports issuance.
questions.question: Which document collects information from the applicant that the insurer uses to underwrite and issue a policy?

questions.insight: Deposit premium is common in commercial policies rated on payroll, sales, or receipts. It is later adjusted by audit. Close-call: minimum premium is the lowest premium charged regardless of audit.
questions.question: Which premium is paid at policy inception and later adjusted after actual exposure is known?

questions.insight: Premium audits make adjustable premiums accurate. They are common in workers compensation, general liability, and commercial auto. Close-call: underwriting happens before or during issuance; audit finalizes exposure after the term.
questions.question: Which process reviews actual exposures such as payroll or sales after the policy period to adjust premium?

questions.insight: A certificate summarizes coverage, limits, and insurer information for third parties. It does not create coverage beyond the policy. Close-call: endorsement can change coverage; certificate generally cannot.
questions.question: Which document provides evidence that insurance exists but generally does not change policy coverage?

questions.insight: TRIA is a federal terrorism insurance backstop for certain commercial lines. Insurers must follow disclosure and offer requirements. Close-call: war exclusions are separate from TRIA-certified terrorism treatment.
questions.question: Which federal program concerns certified acts of terrorism and requires disclosure/offer procedures for certain commercial P&C coverage?

questions.insight: HO-2 covers dwelling and personal property for listed broad-form perils. Important as a named-perils contrast to HO-3. Close-call: HO-3 usually covers the dwelling on open perils and personal property on named perils.
questions.question: Which homeowners form is a named-perils form broader than basic coverage but narrower than HO-3?

questions.insight: HO-3 is essential: Coverage A dwelling is generally open perils subject to exclusions, while Coverage C personal property is named perils. Close-call: HO-5 is broader because personal property is typically open perils too.
questions.question: Which homeowners form is the most common owner-occupied form, typically open perils on the dwelling and named perils on personal property?

questions.insight: HO-4 is renters insurance. It covers the tenant's personal property, loss of use, personal liability, and medical payments. Close-call: HO-6 is for condominium unit owners; HO-3 is for dwelling owners.
questions.question: Which homeowners form is designed for renters/tenants and covers personal property and liability but not the building?

questions.insight: HO-5 is broader than HO-3 because personal property is generally open perils, subject to exclusions. It is important for form-comparison questions. Close-call: HO-3 personal property is usually named perils.
questions.question: Which homeowners form generally provides open-perils coverage for both dwelling and personal property?

questions.insight: HO-6 covers personal property, liability, loss of use, and building items the unit owner is responsible for under the condo agreement. Close-call: the condo association master policy covers common elements and possibly building structure depending on bylaws.
questions.question: Which homeowners form is designed for condominium unit owners?

questions.insight: HO-8 is designed for older homes and often uses modified/functional repair cost concepts. It is narrower than HO-3 and commonly named peril. Close-call: HO-5 is broadest among common owner-occupied forms; HO-8 is specialized.
questions.question: Which homeowners form is intended for older homes where replacement cost may greatly exceed market value?

questions.insight: Coverage A insures the dwelling and attached structures. It drives many other percentage-based limits. Close-call: Coverage B is other structures; Coverage C is personal property.
questions.question: Which homeowners coverage applies to the residence building itself?

questions.insight: Coverage B covers structures separated from the dwelling by clear space. Often excluded or limited if used for business. Close-call: attached garage is Coverage A, not Coverage B.
questions.question: Which homeowners coverage applies to detached structures on the residence premises, such as a detached garage or fence?

questions.insight: Coverage C covers personal property, often worldwide but with limits for certain categories and off-premises property. Close-call: real property/building is Coverage A or B, not C.
questions.question: Which homeowners coverage applies to the insured's movable personal property or contents?

questions.insight: Coverage D handles indirect loss from covered property damage. It matters when the insured must live elsewhere. Close-call: Coverage C pays for contents; Coverage D pays extra living/fair rental expenses.
questions.question: Which homeowners coverage pays additional living expenses or fair rental value after a covered loss makes the residence unusable?

questions.insight: Coverage E is third-party liability coverage. It may include defense. Close-call: Coverage F medical payments may pay regardless of fault but is limited.
questions.question: Which homeowners coverage protects against covered claims that the insured is legally liable for bodily injury or property damage to others?

questions.topic: Coverage F - Medical Payments to Others
questions.insight: Coverage F is goodwill/no-fault medical payments coverage for others, not the insured's household. Close-call: Coverage E requires legal liability; Coverage F usually does not.
questions.question: Which homeowners coverage pays limited medical expenses for others injured on the insured location or by insured activities, regardless of legal liability?

choices.B: Coverage F - Medical Payments to Others
choices.C: Building and Personal Property Coverage Form

questions.insight: DP-1 generally covers basic named perils and may settle losses more narrowly unless endorsed. Important as the minimum dwelling form. Close-call: DP-2 is broader named perils; DP-3 is special/open perils on dwelling.
questions.question: Which dwelling form is the basic named-perils form, often providing the narrowest dwelling-policy coverage?

questions.insight: DP-2 is a named-perils broad form. It is more protective than DP-1 and may include perils such as falling objects or weight of ice/snow depending on form. Close-call: DP-3 is open perils for dwelling unless excluded.
questions.question: Which dwelling form adds broad named perils beyond DP-1 but is still not as broad as DP-3?

questions.insight: DP-3 is the broadest standard dwelling form, commonly used for rental dwellings. It covers the building open perils while personal property may remain named perils. Close-call: HO-3 is for owner-occupied homes with homeowners liability package.
questions.question: Which dwelling form generally covers the dwelling on an open-perils basis subject to exclusions?

questions.insight: Dwelling policies focus on property coverage for dwellings and may not automatically package the same liability and personal property protections as homeowners. Close-call: homeowners policies are package policies for eligible owner-occupants or residents.
questions.question: Which property policy type is often used for residential property that does not qualify for a homeowners form, such as rental dwellings?

choices.C: Building and Personal Property Coverage Form

questions.insight: A CPP packages commercial coverage parts such as property, general liability, inland marine, crime, or auto. It is flexible for businesses. Close-call: BOP is a standardized package for eligible small/medium businesses.
questions.question: Which policy arrangement combines two or more commercial coverage parts under common conditions and declarations?

questions.insight: Commercial property coverage is first-party coverage for business property. It commonly uses building and business personal property forms plus causes of loss forms. Close-call: CGL covers third-party liability, not the insured's own building.
questions.question: Which coverage protects business buildings and business personal property from covered causes of loss?

questions.topic: Building and Personal Property Coverage Form
questions.insight: This form is the core commercial property form. It defines covered property, property not covered, limits, and valuation. Close-call: causes of loss forms determine which perils are covered.
questions.question: Which commercial property form covers the described building, business personal property, and sometimes property of others at the premises?

choices.D: Building and Personal Property Coverage Form

questions.insight: BPP is movable property used in the business. It is usually covered at or near the described premises subject to limits. Close-call: building coverage applies to the structure; BPP applies to contents.
questions.question: Which commercial property category includes business-owned contents, inventory, furniture, fixtures, machinery, and similar property?

questions.insight: Property of others may be covered under commercial property forms but often requires careful limits and valuation. Close-call: bailee coverage/inland marine may be more appropriate for businesses primarily holding others' property.
questions.question: Which commercial property category covers property in the insured's care, custody, or control that belongs to someone else?

questions.insight: Basic causes commonly include fire, lightning, explosion, windstorm/hail, smoke, aircraft/vehicles, riot/civil commotion, vandalism, sprinkler leakage, sinkhole collapse, and volcanic action depending on form. Close-call: broad form adds more named perils; special form is open perils subject to exclusions.
questions.question: Which commercial property causes-of-loss form provides the narrowest named-perils coverage?

questions.insight: Broad form is still named perils but wider than basic. Common additions may include falling objects, weight of snow/ice/sleet, and water damage from certain systems. Close-call: special form is not named-perils; it covers direct physical loss unless excluded.
questions.question: Which commercial property causes-of-loss form adds additional named perils to the basic form?

questions.insight: Special form is the broad/open-perils commercial property form. Always apply exclusions after the broad grant. Close-call: do not call it all-risk without recognizing exclusions.
questions.question: Which commercial property causes-of-loss form covers direct physical loss unless excluded or limited?

questions.insight: Business income is indirect loss coverage. It requires covered direct physical loss causing suspension during the period of restoration. Close-call: extra expense pays costs to reduce downtime; business income pays lost income/continuing expenses.
questions.question: Which coverage pays lost net income and continuing expenses when operations are suspended because of covered property damage?

choices.D: Building and Personal Property Coverage Form

questions.insight: Extra expense helps a business continue operations, such as renting temporary space or equipment. It may reduce business income loss. Close-call: business income reimburses lost income; extra expense reimburses added costs.
questions.question: Which coverage pays necessary additional expenses incurred to avoid or minimize suspension of business after a covered loss?

questions.insight: Equipment breakdown fills gaps left by standard property forms for mechanical/electrical pressure breakdowns. It can include property damage, business income, and spoilage options. Close-call: wear and tear or maintenance failure alone is not the same as covered breakdown.
questions.question: Which coverage addresses sudden and accidental breakdown of covered equipment, such as boilers, machinery, or electrical systems?

questions.insight: Builders risk covers property during construction, often including materials, fixtures, and temporary structures. It usually ends when construction is complete or the property is occupied. Close-call: CGL covers liability at a construction site; builders risk covers the project property.
questions.question: Which coverage insures buildings or structures while under construction?

questions.insight: BOP is a streamlined package with property, business income/extra expense, and liability features. Eligibility depends on business type, size, and risk. Close-call: CPP is more flexible for larger or more complex businesses.
questions.question: Which package policy combines property and liability coverage for eligible small or medium businesses?

questions.insight: Inland marine fills gaps for mobile or specialized property. It often uses floaters. Close-call: ocean marine covers sea transit; commercial property is usually location-based.
questions.question: Which property insurance category covers movable property, property in transit, instruments of transportation/communication, or property of specialized nature?

choices.D: Building and Personal Property Coverage Form

questions.insight: A personal articles floater provides broader scheduled coverage for valuable items that may be limited under homeowners. Close-call: Coverage C covers personal property generally but may have sublimits and named-perils limitations.
questions.question: Which personal inland marine form schedules valuable personal property such as jewelry, cameras, musical instruments, or fine arts?

questions.insight: Commercial floaters cover mobile property such as contractors equipment, installation property, or accounts receivable depending on form. Close-call: building and personal property form is tied to described premises.
questions.question: Which inland marine coverage insures business property that moves from place to place or needs broader off-premises coverage?

questions.insight: NFIP provides flood insurance because standard property policies commonly exclude flood. Important in Texas because flood is a major catastrophic exposure. Close-call: water damage from a burst pipe is not the same as flood.
questions.question: Which federal program is associated with standard flood insurance coverage for eligible communities?

questions.insight: Flood is commonly excluded under homeowners and commercial property policies unless flood coverage is purchased. Parameters matter because seepage, sewer backup, and pipe burst are different concepts. Close-call: wind-driven rain through a storm-created opening is not necessarily flood.
questions.question: Which peril generally means surface water, overflow of inland or tidal waters, or mudflow inundating normally dry land?

questions.insight: Earthquake is commonly excluded in standard property forms and requires endorsement or separate policy. It can include volcanic eruption in some contexts but policy wording controls. Close-call: sinkhole collapse may be treated separately in certain forms.
questions.question: Which coverage is usually added separately to cover earth movement losses?

questions.insight: Mobile home policies adapt homeowners concepts to mobile/manufactured housing. Important because eligibility and valuation differ from standard homes. Close-call: HO-3 is for eligible site-built owner-occupied homes.
questions.question: Which property coverage is designed for manufactured or mobile homes and related personal property/liability needs?

questions.insight: Watercraft coverage may be by endorsement or separate policy, especially for larger boats or higher horsepower. Close-call: homeowners usually provides very limited watercraft coverage.
questions.question: Which coverage addresses boats and related liability/physical damage exposures beyond limited homeowners coverage?

questions.insight: Farm owners policies combine personal and farm property/liability exposures. Close-call: homeowners policies are not designed for commercial farming exposures.
questions.question: Which package is designed for farm dwellings, personal property, farm personal property, barns, and farm liability exposures?

questions.insight: Windstorm covers wind/hail damage when included or separately purchased. In coastal Texas, standard policies may exclude wind/hail and TWIA may be needed. Close-call: flood from storm surge is not windstorm.
questions.question: Which coverage is especially important for Texas coastal property where wind and hail may require separate coverage through TWIA?

questions.insight: First-party cyber pays the insured's own cyber-related expenses, not liability to others. Close-call: third-party cyber liability covers claims by others for privacy/security failures.
questions.question: Which coverage helps the insured with its own cyber loss costs, such as data restoration, breach response, or cyber extortion?

questions.insight: Ordinance or law covers demolition, increased construction costs, or undamaged portions when required by code, subject to coverage parts. Close-call: replacement cost alone may not cover code upgrades.
questions.question: Which property endorsement addresses increased costs caused by enforcement of building codes after covered damage?

questions.insight: Debris removal is an additional property coverage with limits/conditions. It matters after fire, wind, or other covered physical losses. Close-call: pollutant cleanup may be separately limited or excluded.
questions.question: Which additional coverage pays to remove debris of covered property after a covered loss?

questions.insight: CGL is foundational commercial liability coverage. It is third-party coverage, commonly with Coverage A, B, C, supplementary payments, and limits. Close-call: commercial property covers the insured's own property.
questions.question: Which commercial policy covers many business premises/operations, products-completed operations, and personal/advertising injury liability exposures?

questions.insight: Premises/operations covers ongoing operations and conditions on the premises. Example: customer slips in a store. Close-call: products/completed operations applies after products are sold or work is completed.
questions.question: Which CGL exposure involves injuries or damage arising from the insured's location or ongoing business activities?

questions.insight: Products-completed operations covers liability from goods or completed work away from owned/rented premises. It is subject to aggregate limits. Close-call: premises/operations covers ongoing operations.
questions.question: Which CGL exposure involves injury or damage after a product is sold or work is completed?

questions.insight: Coverage A is the core CGL BI/PD liability coverage. It may be occurrence or claims-made. Close-call: Coverage B handles personal and advertising injury; Coverage C handles medical payments.
questions.question: Which CGL coverage applies to bodily injury and property damage liability caused by an occurrence, subject to exclusions?

questions.insight: Coverage B handles specified offenses rather than ordinary bodily injury/property damage. Close-call: personal injury here is not auto PIP; it is a CGL offense category.
questions.question: Which CGL coverage applies to personal and advertising injury such as libel, slander, false arrest, or copyright-related advertising injury?

questions.insight: Coverage C medical payments is no-fault/goodwill coverage with lower limits. Close-call: Coverage A requires legal liability for bodily injury or property damage.
questions.question: Which CGL coverage pays limited medical expenses for injuries on the premises or because of operations, regardless of fault?

questions.insight: Occurrence form focuses on when the injury/damage occurs. It is common in CGL. Close-call: claims-made focuses on when the claim is first made and reported.
questions.question: Which liability trigger covers injury or damage that occurs during the policy period, regardless of when the claim is made?

questions.insight: Claims-made coverage is common in professional liability and some CGL forms. The retroactive date is critical. Close-call: occurrence coverage depends on when injury/damage happened.
questions.question: Which liability trigger covers claims first made during the policy period, subject to retroactive date and reporting rules?

questions.insight: The retroactive date prevents coverage for prior acts before that date. It is one of the most important claims-made concepts. Close-call: policy period is not enough; the act must be after the retro date and claim made/reported properly.
questions.question: Which claims-made feature bars coverage for events occurring before a specified date even if the claim is made during the policy period?

questions.insight: Per occurrence limit caps payment for one occurrence under Coverage A and often Coverage C. Close-call: aggregate is the total for the policy period.
questions.question: Which CGL limit is the most payable for one occurrence before aggregate limits are considered?

questions.insight: The general aggregate caps total payments for covered claims subject to it. It is important for repeated or multiple claims. Close-call: products-completed operations may have its own aggregate.
questions.question: Which CGL limit is the total payable for certain claims during the policy period?

questions.insight: Liability policies respond to covered third-party property damage. Close-call: first-party property coverage pays for the insured's own property.
questions.question: Which liability concept involves covered damage the insured causes to someone else's property, not the insured's own property?

questions.insight: PAP is the key personal lines auto form. It distinguishes liability, med payments/PIP, UM/UIM, and physical damage. Close-call: business auto covers commercial auto exposures.
questions.question: Which policy covers eligible private passenger auto liability, medical payments, UM/UIM, and physical damage coverages?

questions.insight: Business auto coverage uses covered auto symbols to define which autos apply to which coverages. Close-call: personal auto policy is not designed for regular business auto exposures.
questions.question: Which commercial form covers liability and physical damage for autos used in business?

questions.insight: BI liability pays covered injury damages to third parties and may provide defense. Close-call: medical payments/PIP can pay insured/passenger expenses regardless of fault.
questions.question: Which auto liability coverage applies to injuries to others for which the insured is legally responsible?

questions.insight: PD liability pays for damage to others' vehicles or property. In Texas, minimum auto liability includes a property damage limit. Close-call: collision covers damage to the insured auto.
questions.question: Which auto liability coverage applies to damage to another person's property caused by the insured?

questions.insight: Split limits are shown as three numbers, e.g., 30/60/25. They cap different parts of a claim separately. Close-call: combined single limit uses one overall limit per accident.
questions.question: Which auto limit structure shows separate limits for BI per person, BI per accident, and PD per accident?

questions.insight: CSL provides one pool for bodily injury and property damage combined. It can be more flexible than split limits. Close-call: split limits have separate per-person, per-accident, and property damage caps.
questions.question: Which auto limit structure uses one limit for all covered BI and PD in an accident?

questions.insight: Medical payments is first-party/no-fault auto coverage for medical expenses. Close-call: PIP in Texas is broader because it also covers lost wages and other nonmedical costs.
questions.question: Which auto coverage pays reasonable medical/funeral expenses for the insured and passengers regardless of fault, subject to limits?

questions.insight: Collision covers upset or impact with another vehicle/object. It usually has a deductible. Close-call: other-than-collision/comprehensive covers theft, fire, hail, flood, vandalism, or animal impact depending on wording.
questions.question: Which auto physical damage coverage applies when a covered auto overturns or collides with another object?

questions.insight: Other-than-collision is often called comprehensive. It covers non-collision physical damage causes listed or defined in the policy. Close-call: collision is overturn or impact with an object/vehicle.
questions.question: Which auto physical damage coverage applies to losses such as theft, fire, windstorm, hail, flood, vandalism, or hitting an animal?

questions.insight: Specified perils is narrower than comprehensive/other-than-collision. It pays only for listed causes such as fire, theft, explosion, windstorm, hail, flood, mischief, or transportation perils depending on form. Close-call: collision remains separate.
questions.question: Which auto physical damage option covers only listed non-collision perils rather than broad comprehensive causes?

questions.insight: UM responds when the legally liable driver lacks insurance. In Texas it must be offered and rejected in writing if not wanted. Close-call: UIM applies when the at-fault driver has insurance but not enough.
questions.question: Which auto coverage protects the insured when the at-fault driver has no liability insurance or in certain hit-and-run situations?

questions.insight: UIM fills the gap when the other driver's limits are too low, subject to the insured's UIM terms. Close-call: UM is for no insurance; UIM is not enough insurance.
questions.question: Which auto coverage protects the insured when the at-fault driver's liability limits are insufficient?

questions.insight: Owned autos are listed or otherwise qualify as owned covered autos. Close-call: hired autos are borrowed/leased/rented; non-owned autos are not owned, hired, or borrowed by the named insured.
questions.question: Which auto category means a vehicle owned by the insured and covered under the policy?

questions.insight: Non-owned auto coverage is important for employer liability when employees use personal cars for business. Close-call: hired autos are rented/leased/borrowed by the business.
questions.question: Which auto category means an auto not owned by the insured but used in connection with the insured's affairs, often employee-owned autos in business use?

questions.insight: Hired auto coverage is common in business auto. It is distinct from owned autos and employee-owned non-owned autos. Close-call: rental car used personally may be handled by PAP/rental agreements.
questions.question: Which auto category means an auto the insured leases, hires, rents, or borrows?

questions.insight: Temporary substitute autos keep coverage functioning while the covered auto is unavailable. Close-call: newly acquired auto is a vehicle the insured obtains ownership of, not a temporary replacement.
questions.question: Which auto temporarily replaces a covered auto that is out of normal use because of breakdown, repair, servicing, loss, or destruction?

choices.B: Cyber liability and data breach coverage

questions.insight: Newly acquired auto rules protect against gaps but require notice within policy time limits. Close-call: temporary substitute auto is not newly owned.
questions.question: Which auto category refers to a vehicle the insured becomes owner of during the policy period and that may receive automatic coverage for a limited time?

questions.insight: Transportation expense helps with temporary substitute transportation after covered auto loss. It is subject to daily and maximum limits. Close-call: rental reimbursement may be an optional broader coverage.
questions.question: Which auto coverage pays limited costs such as rental or transportation after a covered physical damage loss?

questions.insight: Rental reimbursement is an optional physical damage-related coverage subject to limits. Close-call: liability for damage to a rental car is a separate issue.
questions.question: Which optional auto coverage pays for a rental car while the covered auto is being repaired after a covered loss?

questions.insight: Garagekeepers coverage is important for auto dealers, repair shops, and parking operations. It may be legal liability, direct primary, or direct excess. Close-call: garage liability covers BI/PD from garage operations, not customers' autos.
questions.question: Which coverage protects a garage business for damage to customers' autos in its care, custody, or control?

questions.insight: Auto dealers form combines aspects of business auto and garage liability exposures. Close-call: garagekeepers specifically addresses customers' autos in custody.
questions.question: Which commercial auto form is designed for dealerships and similar auto businesses, including owned autos and garage exposures?

questions.insight: DOC fills personal-use gaps for individuals furnished company cars. Close-call: non-owned auto coverage for a business does not necessarily protect an executive's personal exposure as broadly.
questions.question: Which endorsement provides personal-auto-like coverage for named individuals who have a company auto but no personal auto policy?

questions.insight: Mobile equipment is usually handled under CGL rather than auto unless subject to financial responsibility laws or used on public roads in certain ways. Close-call: autos designed for road use fall under auto coverage.
questions.question: Which term describes land vehicles not considered autos because they are designed mainly for off-road use or specialized operations?

choices.A: Cyber liability and data breach coverage

questions.insight: Workers comp pays benefits required by workers compensation law, regardless of employer negligence. It is generally exclusive remedy. Close-call: employers liability covers certain employee injury suits outside workers comp benefits.
questions.question: Which coverage provides statutory benefits to employees for work-related injuries or occupational disease?

questions.insight: Employers liability is Part Two of the workers compensation policy. It responds to covered suits by employees or related third parties. Close-call: workers comp benefits are statutory first-party employee benefits.
questions.question: Which coverage protects the employer against certain employee injury lawsuits not fully covered by workers compensation benefits?

questions.insight: Exclusive remedy limits employee lawsuits when workers comp applies. It is central to the workers comp bargain. Close-call: third-party suits and some exceptions may still occur.
questions.question: Which doctrine generally makes workers compensation the employee's sole remedy against the employer for covered work injuries?

questions.insight: Other states insurance addresses incidental exposures in states where the employer may begin operations. It requires proper listing and notice. Close-call: it is not a substitute for known regular operations in another state.
questions.question: Which workers compensation feature extends coverage for work in states listed in the policy's other-states section?

questions.insight: Workers comp premium is heavily driven by employee class codes and payroll, adjusted by experience rating where applicable. Close-call: general liability may use sales, area, or payroll depending on class.
questions.question: Which rating basis commonly uses job classifications, payroll, rates, and experience modification?

questions.insight: Employee dishonesty covers dishonest acts by employees, often subject to discovery and employee definitions. Close-call: theft by outsiders may require theft/burglary/robbery coverage.
questions.question: Which crime coverage protects against theft of money, securities, or property by employees?

questions.insight: Theft is the broad taking concept and may include burglary or robbery depending on form definitions. Close-call: burglary requires unlawful entry; robbery involves taking from a person by force or threat.
questions.question: Which crime term broadly means unlawful taking of property?

questions.insight: Robbery involves a person/victim and force or threat. Close-call: burglary is unlawful entry into premises, not necessarily confrontation.
questions.question: Which crime term means taking property from a person by force or threat of force?

questions.insight: Burglary focuses on unlawful entry into premises. Policy definitions may require marks of forced entry. Close-call: robbery involves taking from a person by force/threat.
questions.question: Which crime term means unlawful entry into premises with intent to commit a crime, often requiring visible signs of forcible entry or exit in insurance forms?

questions.insight: Forgery/alteration coverage protects against financial instrument manipulation. Close-call: employee dishonesty may apply if an employee commits theft, but forgery coverage has specific instrument focus.
questions.question: Which crime coverage addresses forged or altered checks, drafts, promissory notes, or similar instruments?

questions.insight: Mysterious disappearance is often excluded or limited because the cause cannot be verified. Close-call: theft requires evidence of unlawful taking, depending on policy wording.
questions.question: Which crime/property term describes property missing without known cause or explanation?

questions.insight: Surety bonds involve principal, obligee, and surety. The surety expects reimbursement from the principal if it pays. Close-call: insurance generally has two parties and spreads risk; surety guarantees performance.
questions.question: Which bond involves three parties and guarantees the principal's performance or obligation to the obligee?

questions.insight: Fidelity bonds are similar to employee dishonesty coverage. They protect against dishonesty rather than guaranteeing contract performance. Close-call: surety bond protects an obligee against principal's failure to perform.
questions.question: Which bond protects an employer or business from dishonest acts of employees?

questions.insight: E&O covers financial injury from professional mistakes, often on claims-made forms. Critical for insurance agents. Close-call: CGL Coverage A usually requires bodily injury/property damage, not pure professional financial loss.
questions.question: Which professional liability coverage protects against claims alleging negligent professional services or failure to perform professional duties?

questions.insight: Medical malpractice is a professional liability form for medical services. It is often claims-made and has specialized consent/defense features. Close-call: general liability covers premises slips, not professional treatment errors.
questions.question: Which professional liability coverage applies to healthcare professional negligence?

questions.insight: D&O addresses managerial decisions and governance claims. It may protect individuals and reimburse the organization. Close-call: EPLI covers employment-practices claims; E&O covers professional services.
questions.question: Which liability coverage protects directors and officers against claims alleging wrongful management acts?

questions.insight: EPLI covers workplace practice claims by employees or applicants. Close-call: workers compensation covers work injuries; EPLI covers employment-law allegations.
questions.question: Which coverage protects against employment-related claims such as discrimination, wrongful termination, or harassment?

questions.topic: Cyber liability and data breach coverage
questions.insight: Cyber coverage may include first-party and third-party insuring agreements. Close-call: CGL may not cover data/privacy losses due to electronic data exclusions and non-BI/PD nature.
questions.question: Which coverage addresses liability and expenses arising from data breaches, privacy failures, cyber events, or funds transfer fraud?

choices.B: Cyber liability and data breach coverage

questions.insight: Liquor liability is needed by bars, restaurants, and sellers/servers of alcohol. Close-call: host liquor may be included for incidental non-business alcohol exposures, but businesses selling alcohol need liquor liability.
questions.question: Which coverage protects businesses against claims arising from serving or furnishing alcohol?

questions.insight: Umbrella liability sits above primary policies and may broaden some coverage. It is critical for catastrophic liability losses. Close-call: excess liability usually follows underlying coverage more closely.
questions.question: Which policy provides high-limit liability coverage over underlying policies and may sometimes cover certain gaps subject to self-insured retention?

questions.insight: Excess liability increases limits after underlying limits are exhausted. Close-call: umbrella may provide broader coverage; excess typically follows form.
questions.question: Which policy provides additional limits above underlying insurance, often following the same terms as the underlying policy?

questions.insight: The Texas General Lines Property and Casualty path includes a 100-scoreable-question general knowledge portion plus 10 pretest questions and a 30-scoreable-question state-specific portion plus 5 pretest questions. Pretest questions are unscored. Close-call: personal lines and adjuster exams use different outlines.
questions.question: Which statement best describes the Texas P&C agent exam structure in the Pearson outline?

questions.insight: The Commissioner leads TDI insurance regulation, including licensing, examinations, investigations, rules, orders, and enforcement. Close-call: the insurer, not the commissioner, issues policies; TDI regulates market conduct and solvency.
questions.question: Which Texas official is responsible for administering and enforcing the Texas Insurance Code and regulating the insurance business in Texas?

questions.insight: Record examinations support enforcement and market conduct oversight. Agents must maintain required records and make them available as required. Close-call: investigation/hearing is broader enforcement process after possible violations.
questions.question: Which regulatory power allows TDI/Commissioner to inspect insurer or licensee books and records to determine compliance?

questions.insight: Investigations gather facts; hearings provide due process before certain sanctions. Important because license discipline requires procedural protections. Close-call: cease and desist orders may be used to stop ongoing violations.
questions.question: Which regulatory process is used when TDI investigates possible violations and gives required notice before formal administrative action?

questions.insight: Administrative penalties are regulatory fines or sanctions. The outline flags penalties as a state-specific topic; always verify current statutory amounts for exact penalty questions. Close-call: license suspension/revocation affects authority to transact insurance; a penalty is monetary or administrative.
questions.question: Which sanction may be assessed by TDI/Commissioner for violations of insurance laws or rules?

questions.insight: A cease and desist order is used to halt prohibited conduct. Violating such an order can lead to additional sanctions. Close-call: license revocation removes license authority; cease and desist tells the party to stop specific conduct.
questions.question: Which order tells a person or company to stop an unlawful insurance practice?

questions.insight: A certificate of authority is insurer-level authorization from the state. Close-call: an agent license authorizes a person/agency to act as agent; it does not authorize an insurer to operate.
questions.question: Which authorization allows an insurer to transact insurance in Texas?

questions.insight: Transacting insurance is broadly regulated. A license may be required before soliciting, negotiating, selling, or servicing insurance. Close-call: providing general information may be different, but exam questions usually test licensed insurance activity.
questions.question: Which concept includes acts such as soliciting, negotiating, making, or delivering insurance contracts under Texas regulatory jurisdiction?

questions.insight: Domestic means formed under Texas law. Close-call: foreign means organized under another U.S. state; alien means organized under another country.
questions.question: Which insurer is organized under the laws of Texas?

questions.insight: Foreign insurer does not mean outside the U.S.; it means outside Texas but within another U.S. jurisdiction. Close-call: alien insurer is organized under another country.
questions.question: Which insurer is organized under the laws of another U.S. state or jurisdiction, not Texas?

questions.insight: Alien insurer means non-U.S. insurer. Close-call: foreign insurer means another state, not another country.
questions.question: Which insurer is organized under the laws of a country other than the United States?

questions.insight: A stock insurer is owned by stockholders and generally operates for profit. Close-call: mutual insurer is owned by policyholders.
questions.question: Which insurer is owned by shareholders?

questions.insight: A mutual insurer is owned by policyholders and may pay dividends when declared. Close-call: dividends are not guaranteed; stock insurers are owned by shareholders.
questions.question: Which insurer is owned by policyholders?

choices.C: General Lines Property and Casualty Agent

questions.insight: Admitted insurers have state authorization/certificate of authority. They are subject to Texas regulation and guaranty mechanisms as applicable. Close-call: nonadmitted/surplus lines insurers are not admitted but may lawfully write certain risks through surplus lines rules.
questions.question: Which insurer is authorized by Texas to transact insurance in the state?

choices.B: General Lines Property and Casualty Agent

questions.insight: Nonadmitted does not automatically mean illegal; surplus lines rules can allow placement when admitted markets are unavailable. Close-call: unauthorized illegal insurance is not the same as lawful surplus lines placement.
